tiers.name: small
tiers.goal: trusted anchor tasks with direct hidden-oracle benches or formal collateral

tiers.name: medium
tiers.goal: single-IP manuals and protocol slices that still fit into one verification episode
tiers.acquisition: prefer official PDF docs; otherwise render authoritative HTML docs to PDF during ingest

tiers.name: large
tiers.goal: subsystem, core, controller, and SoC-level manuals with richer integration constraints
tiers.acquisition: repository-linked user manuals, top-level datasheets, and open protocol standards

tiers.name: industrial
tiers.goal: real standards and SoC architecture packages that require retrieval, chunking, and legal review
tiers.acquisition: treat public and license-gated sources separately

datasets.name: rtllm_v1_1
datasets.notes: Small hand-written designs with explicit descriptions, a hidden simulation bench, and a verified RTL reference.

datasets.name: verilogeval_v2_spec_to_rtl
datasets.notes: Spec-to-RTL benchmark with the improved VerilogEval v2 harness and structured failure analysis.

datasets.name: rtllm_v2_0
datasets.notes: Expanded RTLLM benchmark with categorized tasks; good for widening anchor-task families before scaling.

datasets.name: asserteval
datasets.notes: Use this to train assertion and formal-task generation, not as the main generator benchmark. The current AssertLLM repo README says 20 designs; the later OpenLLM-RTL paper summary says 18, so verify the checkout you actually use.

datasets.name: opentitan_dv
datasets.notes: Repository-native industrial verification artifacts for later agentic verification benchmarks.

datasets.name: opentitan_ip_docs
datasets.notes: First curated pack is checked into the repo as manual task bundles for uart, i2c, spi_host, dma, and sysrst_ctrl from the local OpenTitan checkout. GPIO was not present in this checkout, so sysrst_ctrl is the fifth medium-tier task for now.

datasets.name: openhw_core_ip_manuals
datasets.notes: Use focused OpenHW manuals or manual chapters, especially CORE-V single-core manuals, as medium-sized architecture specs. These are a good bridge from toy tasks to real manuals because they already point at mature RTL and verification repos.

datasets.name: wishbone_b4
datasets.notes: Use Wishbone B4 as an open protocol-spec family. It is useful for medium-tier interface tasks once paired with OpenCores or LiteX-compatible implementations and hand-built oracle benches.

datasets.name: opentitan_top_docs
datasets.notes: Use OpenTitan top-level datasheets such as Earl Grey and Darjeeling for large-tier SoC-style specifications. These give a real integration story while staying fully public and repo-linked.

datasets.name: cva6_user_manual
datasets.notes: Use the CVA6 user manual family as a large public CPU-core corpus. It is large enough to force retrieval/chunking, but still connected to public RTL and verification infrastructure.

datasets.name: core_v_mcu_user_manual
fields: user manual PDF or rendered HTML
datasets.notes: Use CORE-V-MCU as a public large-tier SoC manual with multiple peripherals, DMA, and subsystem integration concerns.

datasets.name: nvdla_docs
datasets.notes: Use NVDLA as a large public accelerator-scale corpus. The docs are paired with RTL, a reference testbench, sanity tests, and synthesis material, which makes it unusually useful for verifier-agent work.

datasets.name: onfi_specs
datasets.notes: Use ONFI protocol specs as the open large-tier analogue to DDR-style memory-interface work. They are downloadable public PDFs and cover high-speed NAND interfaces with real timing/protocol complexity.

datasets.name: rtl_repo
datasets.notes: Repository-level scale corpus for context conditioning and code exposure, not a trustworthy reward oracle.

datasets.name: jedec_ddr3_private
datasets.notes: Use this only once legal access is settled. DDR3-class standards are exactly the kind of industrial corpora you want, but they should sit in a separate private pipeline, not the public dataset path.

datasets.name: jedec_ddr6_private
datasets.notes: Treat DDR6 and similar next-generation standards as a separate industrial-private tier. They are valuable long-term targets, but not good public bootstrap data because the standards and collateral are access-controlled.
